one_line_takeaway: A purely theoretical (non-security) paper that extends the 'language generation in the limit' framework with a replay adversary modeling model-collapse feedback loops, proving that replay leaves uniform generation and countable-class generation-in-the-limit unaffected but strictly and provably harms non-uniform generation, general-class generation-in-the-limit, and proper generation-in-the-limit.
key_finding: Whether replay (the generator being fed its own past outputs) makes language generation harder is highly non-uniform: it is completely benign for the strongest guarantee (uniform generation, any hypothesis class) and for generation-in-the-limit over countable classes (both provably equivalent to the standard, no-replay setting), but it creates strict, provable separations for non-uniform generation (even over some countable classes), for generation-in-the-limit over general/uncountable classes, and for proper generation-in-the-limit (even over some finite classes). The authors note their positive (replay-is-benign) results formally mirror practical heuristics such as data cleaning, watermarking, and output filtering, while their negative (separation) results indicate the theoretical limits of when such heuristics can fail to prevent collapse-like degradation.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'language generation with replay,' an extension of Kleinberg and Mullainathan's (2024) 'language generation in the limit' game-theoretic framework, designed to model the feedback loop underlying model collapse. In the standard game, an adversary reveals a stream of valid examples from a hidden target language h*, and a generator must eventually output only novel, valid elements. The replay variant lets the adversary additionally inject the generator's own past outputs (potentially invalid ones) back into the stream, modeling synthetic content re-entering a training corpus. The paper gives a fine-grained characterization, across four notions of generatability (uniform, non-uniform, in-the-limit, and proper-in-the-limit) and three hypothesis-class regimes (finite, countable, general), of exactly when replay changes what is achievable relative to the standard (no-replay) setting. Concretely: (1) Theorem 4.1 proves uniform generatability is completely unaffected by replay -- any uniform generator can be converted (via a 'burn-in' phase, Algorithm 1) into one that is uniformly generatable with replay at the same sample complexity d*. (2) Theorem 5.1 constructs an explicit countable hypothesis class (built from hypotheses h_n = {1,...,n} union negative integers, and h_infinity = all naturals) that is non-uniformly generatable in the standard setting but NOT non-uniformly generatable under replay -- a strict separation, proved by an adversary that forces the generator to reveal its own outputs as fresh 'replay' evidence, driving a contradiction. (3) Theorem 6.1 gives a constructive membership-query-only algorithm, 'Witness Protection' (WP, Algorithm 2), that generates in the limit under replay any countable hypothesis class, matching the standard-setting guarantee of Kleinberg and Mullainathan (2024) for countable classes. (4) Theorem 6.6 exhibits an uncountable hypothesis class (built from marker tokens *_k and integer supports H^b_1/H^b_2) that IS generatable in the limit without replay but is provably NOT generatable in the limit with replay -- showing replay can be fundamentally more restrictive once classes are uncountable; the separation is shown to hinge on as little as a single replayed example. (5) Theorem 7.1 is a standalone (non-replay) computational lower bound: no deterministic generator using only membership queries can properly generate all countable classes in the limit -- additional access (e.g. subset queries) is necessary, strengthening a result of Kleinberg and Mullainathan (2024). (6) Theorem 7.3 (proof not in the retrieved text but stated in the results table and body) shows a finite hypothesis class that is properly generatable in the limit in the standard setting but not under replay, i.e. proper generation fails under replay even for finite classes.
threat_model: Not applicable / not a security paper. The 'adversary' in this work is the standard worst-case adversary construct from online-learning and language-identification theory (Gold 1967, Angluin 1980, Kleinberg and Mullainathan 2024): it adaptively selects which valid (or replayed) example to reveal each round in order to make the generator's task as hard as possible, with the sole aim of proving lower bounds/impossibility results. There is no notion of a malicious attacker exploiting a deployed system, no data-poisoning or prompt-injection threat, no defender, and no security property (confidentiality/integrity/availability) at stake. The paper is a formal-learning-theory analysis of model collapse (a data-quality/robustness phenomenon), not an AI-security study.
limitations: The paper states these limitations/scope boundaries explicitly or via its framing: (1) the framework abstracts away specific model architectures, training algorithms, and any notion of statistical/sample efficiency in the classical PAC sense -- it is an information-theoretic, worst-case game with computationally unbounded generators (except where computational primitives like membership/subset queries are explicitly discussed in Section 7.1), so results do not translate directly into guarantees for real, resource-bounded LLMs. (2) The replay model assumes the adversary can inject arbitrary past generator outputs at any time and the generator has no way to distinguish organic from replayed examples except via the game history -- a stylized abstraction of real corpus contamination. (3) The paper explicitly contrasts itself with 'robust generation in the limit' work (Raman and Raman 2025; Bai et al. 2026; Mehrotra et al. 2026) that allows a finite or exogenously-set rate of noisy/omitted examples, noting their own replay contamination rate is instead endogenously determined by the generator's own outputs -- a different and not strictly comparable noise model. (4) No empirical validation is offered or claimed; all claims are asymptotic/limit-based existence and impossibility results.
